title: Formal SignWriting Overview
subtitle: Plain-text encodings, grammar, search, rendering, styling, and implementation
summary: Plain-text encodings, grammar, search, layout, rendering, styling, and implementation practice define the Formal SignWriting technical model.

slides.title: What Formal SignWriting Does
slides.summary: Formal SignWriting defines the text model that lets written signs be stored, searched, laid out, rendered, and processed.

image_prompts.concrete: Technical overview slide showing a SignWriting signbox flowing into text encoding, search, layout, rendering, and implementation
image_prompts.abstract: A structured sign becoming processable text through connected technical layers

slides.speech: Formal SignWriting is the technical series. Its job is to explain how written signs can be stored, checked, searched, laid out, rendered, styled, and implemented without collapsing canonical text, layout, and presentation into one layer.
slides.notes: Open with function. This is the technical mechanism series.

slides.title: Layer Boundaries
slides.summary: The series keeps canonical text, layout, rendering, styling, search, and implementation distinct.

image_prompts.concrete: Layered architecture slide for Formal SignWriting: canonical text, encoding, grammar, search, layout, rendering, styling, implementation
image_prompts.abstract: Stacked technical layers around a stable written sign

bullets: Canonical text is the durable source
slides.speech: The most important habit in this series is layer discipline. Canonical text is not rendering. Layout is not styling. Search is not authorship. Implementation depends on keeping those boundaries visible.
slides.notes: This slide previews the whole technical architecture.

slides.title: Technical Reading Path
slides.summary: Readers can move from the core Formal SignWriting model into FSW/SWU, signbox structure, grammar, search, layout, rendering, styling, and practice.

image_prompts.concrete: Technical reading path slide moving through Formal SignWriting, FSW and SWU, Shape of a Sign, Grammar, Search, Layout, Rendering, Styling, and Practice
image_prompts.abstract: A technical path through connected specification documents

bullets: Read encodings and signbox structure next
Use grammar, search, and layout for behavior
Use rendering, styling, and practice for implementation
slides.speech: The clearest path begins with the core model, then FSW and SWU, then the signbox. From there readers can move into grammar, search, layout, rendering, styling, and practice depending on what they are trying to implement.
slides.notes: Close as a map for developers and technical reviewers.
